coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots for relative humidity determination and information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Yujing
Lai, Wenqiang
Zheng, Nan
Weng, Wen
coredata.dc:description: The relative humidity (RH) determination is crucial in many fields. Based on the phosphorescent properties of room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon dots, the RTP carbon dots as a probe are expected to be used to rapidly detect relative humidity. In this study, matrix-free room-temperature phosphorescent N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully prepared from urea, succinic acid, and acrylamide using a hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized N-CDs had good biocompatibility and water solubility. The N-CDs emitted blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence. Moreover, the N-CD powder exhibited stable phosphorescence with a phosphorescence lifetime of 158 ms (afterglow time to the naked eye for ~7 s). Because H2O molecules affected the afterglow time, the as-prepared N-CD test paper for the first time could be applied as a probe to monitor RH, the afterglow time of the N-CD test paper is linearly related to the RH (y = −0.0729x+7.042, R2 = 0.998) and the RH detection range is 0%–85%. And the results were consistent with those obtained using a hygrometer. In addition, the N-CD solution could also be used as an encryption ink in the advanced information security field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Relative humidity determination
Security ink
Information encryption

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian (Nos. 2018H0030 ), and the Education Bureau of Fujian (No. JZ160453 ). The funder had no role in study design, in the collection, analysis and interpretation of data, in the writing of the report, and nor in the decision to submit the article for publication.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of N-CDs. (b) TEM and HRTEM images of N-CDs. (c) Particle size distribution of N-CDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs. (b) XPS survey scan of N-CDs. (c) XPS C 1s, (d) XPS N 1s, and (e) XPS O 1s spectra of N-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorbance (red line), PLE, and PL spectra of N-CD solution. Inset: N-CD solution under daylight and UV light (365 nm) irradiation. (b) UV–Vis absorbance (red line), PLE, and PL spectra of N-CD powder. Inset: N-CD powder under daylight and UV light (365 nm) irradiation. (c) Phosphorescence spectrum of N-CD powder. (d) The emission color coordinates of the fluorescence (λex = 370 nm) and phosphorescence (λex = 365 nm) for the solution and powder of N-CDs (1: Fluorescent of N-CDs solution (0.167, 0.160); 2: Fluorescent of N-CDs powder (0.199, 0.210); 3: Phosphorescent of N-CDs powder (0.319, 0.416)). (e) RTP decay of N-CD powder (excitation wavelength: 365 nm; emission wavelength: 528 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Phosphorescence emission images for the N-CDs powder after stopping the UV lamp (365 nm) irradiation for 1 s–7 s at room temperature. (b) Phosphorescence intensities of N-CD powder during continuous UV irradiation (365 nm). Irradiation time: 0–60 min. (c) Phosphorescence emission spectra of N-CD powder in air and N2 (excitation wavelength: 365 nm). (d) Phosphorescence spectra of N-CD powder at different temperatures from 300 to 350 K (excitation wavelength: 365 nm).

(a) The afterglow time images for the N-CDs-based test paper under 298 K and RH 0%, 14%, 42% and 69%. (b) Dependence of phosphorescence lifetime of N-CDs on RH. (c) Change in the afterglow time with four cycles when the relative air humidity (RH) directly increases from 20% RH to 70% RH.

(a) Images of RTP N-CD-based ink printed on filter paper under daylight, under UV light (365 nm), and after removal of the UV light source. (b) Images of N-CD-based ink with RTP and N-CDs-1-based ink with no RTP printed on filter paper under daylight, under UV light (365 nm), and after removal of the UV light source.
Determination of relative air humidity in air using hygrometer (clinical detection) and N-CD-based test paper.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots for relative humidity determination and information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, 363000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, 363000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The relative humidity (RH) determination is crucial in many fields. Based on the phosphorescent properties of room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon dots, the RTP carbon dots as a probe are expected to be used to rapidly detect relative humidity. In this study, matrix-free room-temperature phosphorescent N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully prepared from urea, succinic acid, and acrylamide using a hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized N-CDs had good biocompatibility and water solubility. The N-CDs emitted blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence. Moreover, the N-CD powder exhibited stable phosphorescence with a phosphorescence lifetime of 158 ms (afterglow time to the naked eye for ~7 s). Because H2O molecules affected the afterglow time, the as-prepared N-CD test paper for the first time could be applied as a probe to monitor RH, the afterglow time of the N-CD test paper is linearly related to the RH (y = −0.0729x+7.042, R2 = 0.998) and the RH detection range is 0%–85%. And the results were consistent with those obtained using a hygrometer. In addition, the N-CD solution could also be used as an encryption ink in the advanced information security field.

ce:section.ce:para: Water plays a crucial important role in the life. Therefore, in some cases, quantitative detection of water may be necessary [1]. Especially the relative humidity (RH) determination is in textile, chemical, biological, agriculture, and manufacturing processing [2–4]. Conventionally, we use the mechanical hygrometer, chilled mirror hygrometer, and electronic sensors etc to confirm the RH [5,6]. However, the longer response time, low sensitivity and electromagnetic interference limit their application in some cases. So, it has become a research hotspot in recent years. At present, it is mainly focused on the research of fiber optics sensors. Minghong Yang et al. reported the polyimide coated optics sensors with the high sensitivity and its detection humidity range of 20–95% [7]. G. Salvato et al. presented humidity sensor based on the graphene oxide/Cu junction and investigates its electrical properties [8]. Yutang Dai et al. proposed a relative humidity sensor based on femtosecond laser induced spiral micro-structured single mode-multimode-single mode (FISM-SMS) fiber structure was experimentally demonstrated [9]. However, contrast that with fiber optic sensing, the luminescent method is a powerful and reliable tool due to its high sensitivity, rapidity and ease of operation. And, it is previously reported that the relative humidity determination was based on the fluorescence properties of the organic probes. H. Mishra et al. prepared the sensitivity of fluorescence characteristics of salicylic acid doped poly (vinyl alcohol) film and it has been exploited as a humidity or water sensor [10]. Fernando Pina et al. proposed a polymer hydrogel matrix containing a fluorescent flavylium compound and it can be as a humidity sensor with high sensitivity [11]. But these probes have complex preparation process and the toxic organic solvents. Accordingly, detecting CDs-based RH probe with sensitive, nontoxic, environmentally friendly and inexpensive need to prepare by simple low-cost methods.

Carbon dots (CDs), a type of luminescent carbon nanomaterial, have received increasing interest in the recent years because of their advantages, including simple preparation methods, tunable optical properties, good biocompatibility, and strong fluorescence stability [12–16]. Therefore, CDs have been widely applied in sensing, optoelectronics, catalysis, bioimaging and so on [17–24]. For the sensing, the CDs often used as fluorescent probe for detection heavy metals and environmental analysis [25,26]. But, the application of CDs as a probe to detect RH has not been reported. Because the phosphorescent properties of RTP CDs are related to hydrogen bonding, the H2O molecules can break hydrogen bonds and affect the triplet state stability of the RTP CDs. And the afterglow time to the naked eye is relatively short. Based on this property, we hope to use the RTP CDs as a probe to rapidly detect RH in the air. Notably, CDs have also been reported to exhibit room-temperature phosphorescence. However, it was necessary to embed these CDs in matrices, such as polyvinyl alcohol, polyurethane, urea/biuret, boric acid, and zeolites, to stabilize the excited triplet state [27–32]. In recent years, matrix-free RTP CDs have also been reported [33,34]. The matrix-free RTP CDs possess the advantages of simple preparation process. In order to successfully prepared the matrix-free RTP CDs, we found that the hydrogen bonds and reaction temperature played an important role [35,36].

In this study, the urea, succinic acid, and acrylamide as starting materials were used to prepare the matrix-free RTP nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by a hydrothermal method. Because polyacrylamide formed by acrylamide at 200 °C, which can stabilize excited triplet state species. That can promote the prepared carbon dots to have phosphorescent properties. The experimental data show the as-prepared N-CDs have stable phosphorescence with a phosphorescence lifetime of 158 ms (afterglow time for ~7 s to the naked eye). Test paper prepared based on N-CDs was firstly applied to monitor RH and can accurately detect RH (the RH detection range is 0%–85%). In addition, the N-CDs aqueous solution as a security ink could be used for applications in information encryption and decryption. Therefore, the as-prepared multifunctional RTP N-CDs is not only used for the first time as a probe to detect RH, but can also be used as a secure ink to information encryption.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich or Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ cm−1) was used for all experiments. All solid samples were dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h before sample characterization.

The FEI/Philips Tecnai G2F20 TWIN transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering instrument (Zetasizer Nano ZS, ZEN 3690, Malvern), Bruker Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Hyperion), PerkinElmer UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750), KRATOS Axis ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer recorded the transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, the particle size distribution, the fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, the ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of N-CDs, respectively. The fluorescence and phosphorescence properties of the N-CDs were studied using a luminescence spectrometer (Fluoro Max-4, Horiba Jobin Yvon).

First, 4 g of urea, 1 g of succinic acid, and 4 g of acrylamide were added into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing 15 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was heated at 200 °C for 36 h. After cooling to 25 °C, the solution was dialyzed against deionized water using a 500 Da dialysis bag to remove impurities. Finally, the light brown N-CD solution was freeze-dried to obtain N-CD powder.

N-doped carbon dots with no RTP (N-CDs-1) was synthesized according to a previous report [35]. In brief, 0.5 g of citric acid and 4 g of urea were used as starting materials under the same reaction conditions. After cooling to room-temperature, the bright yellow solution was dialyzed against deionized water using a 500 Da dialysis bag to remove impurities to give the N-CDs-1 solution.

Filter paper was dipped into the N-CD solution (0.03 g mL−1) and the resulting N-CD test paper was dried in a vacuum oven at 30 °C for 24 h. The phosphorescence of the N-CD-based test paper was observed following irradiation with 365 nm UV light for 1 min.

In a confined space, the humidity was artificially regulated, and a hygrometer was used to determine the relative humidity of the space. Then put the as-prepared N-CD-based test paper in the space for about 10 min. After that, the test paper was irradiated with an ultraviolet lamp for 5 min, and turned off the ultraviolet lamp. The afterglow time of the test paper was used a stopwatch to determine, based on the linear relationship between afterglow time and RH. Finally, the RH value was calculated and obtained.

In a typical formulation, 100 mg of N-CDs or N-CDs-1 powder was added to 50 mL of ultrapure water to prepare N-CD-based or N-CDs-1-based ink. The two solutions were injected into an empty commercial inkjet printer cartridge (EPSON Deskjet L 310 printer) and used directly as security printing ink for information encryption and anticounterfeiting.

HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate for 24 h at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Then, different concentrations of N-CDs (0–800 μg mL−1) were added into each well. After 48 h incubation, the relative viabilities of the cell samples were determined using the 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay to assess the metabolic activity of the cells treated with the N-CDs. The cells were lysed using acidulated sodium dodecyl sulfate. The absorbance was recorded at 570 nm using a microplate reader.

First, RTP matrix-free N-CDs were successfully prepared by a hydrothermal method using urea, succinic acid, and acrylamide as starting materials (Fig. 1 a). The morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs was examined using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1b, the N-CDs displayed good dispersion, with an average size of 5 nm. In addition, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the N-CDs showed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset, Fig. 1b), which matches the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100 facet) [34]. As shown by the particle size distribution (Fig. 1c), the N-CDs had diameters of 3–7 nm (average size: ~5 nm), which is consistent with the TEM results. HeLa cells were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the cell viability remained ~90% up to a N-CD concentration of 800 μg mL−1, which indicates that the as-prepared N-CDs have no obvious cytotoxicity.

Next, the structure, elemental composition, and surface composition of the N-CDs were investigated by using FTIR spectroscopy and XPS. The FTIR spectrum of N-CDs shows –OH stretching vibrations and –NH2 stretching vibrations at ~3419 and ~3199 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 2962 and 2920 cm−1 correspond to asymmetric and symmetric methylene (–CH2–) stretching vibrations, respectively [37]. The absorption peaks at 1664, 1641, 1566, 1404, and 1051 cm−1 correspond to CO stretching (amide bonds), N–H bending vibrations, CN stretching vibrations, C–N bending modes, and C–O–C symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 2 a) [38–42]. Thus, the N-CDs contain hydrophilic groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2. The FTIR results were further confirmed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2b, the XPS survey scan indicates that the N-CDs are composed of carbon (65%), oxygen (10%), and nitrogen (25%). The C 1s spectrum of the N-CDs was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6 (C–C/CC), 285.9 (C–O/C–N), 287.7 (CONH), and 288.7 eV (CO/CN) (Fig. 2c). As shown in Fig. 2d, the XPS N 1s spectrum of the N-CDs has two peaks at 399.4 and 401.3 eV, which correspond to C–NC (pyridinic N) and N–H. Previous reports have shown that C–N/CN groups can facilitate RTP [30]. As shown in Fig. 2e, the XPS O 1s spectrum of the N-CDs consists of two peaks corresponding to CO (531.6 eV) and C–O (533.1 eV) bonds.

Subsequently, the optical properties of the N-CDs were investigated. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis absorbance, photoluminescence excitation (PLE), and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the N-CD solution. The UV–Vis absorbance spectrum (red line) had two peaks at 264 and 303 nm, which were attributed to the π–π* transitions of CC groups and the n–π* transitions of CO/CN groups, respectively [43]. The presence of CO/CN groups can improve spin–orbit coupling, thus facilitating intersystem crossing (ISC) and triplet exciton generation [30]. Under UV irradiation (365 nm), the N-CD solution emitted blue fluorescence (inset, Fig. 3a). In addition, the N-CD solution exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence (Fig. S2) with an emission maximum at 418 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. 3a). Moreover, the lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the N-CDs solution were 6.81 ns and 25.2%, respectively (Figs. S3 and S4). The fluorescence properties of the N-CD powder were similar to those of the N-CD solution (Fig. 3b and Fig. S5). Under the optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the emission maximum of the N-CD powder was 440 nm. Furthermore, the N-CD powder emitted blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (365 nm) (inset, Fig. 3b). Owing to aggregation-induced quenching, the lifetime and fluorescence QY of the N-CD powder were lower than those of the N-CD solution (3.09 ns and 9.6%, respectively) (Figs. S6 and S7). The phosphorescence spectrum of the N-CD powder is shown in Fig. 3c. After removal of the UV irradiation source (365 nm), a broad phosphorescence peak (380–800 nm) centered at ~528 nm was observed. In addition, the fluorescence and phosphorescence colors in the chromaticity coordinates were calculated from the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the N-CDs (Fig. 3d). As shown in Fig. 3e, the phosphorescence decay of the N-CD powder could be fitted using a triexponential function, which gave lifetime components of 1.52 ms (6%), 21.31 ms (21%), and 210.91 ms (73%). The average lifetime was calculated to be 158 ms using the following equation:
τ
a v g
= ∑
α i
τ i 2
/ ∑
α i
τ i 2
(under excitation at 365 nm). Furthermore, the N-CDs powder emit blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (365 nm). When the UV light was turned off, the N-CDs powder emits a green phosphorescence and the phosphorescence was readily visible to the naked eye for over 7 s (Fig. 4 a).

Based on the above results, owing to effective ISC and stabilization of the excited triplet species, the N-CD powder exhibited strong RTP. However, because of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, the N-CD powder had weak fluorescence. The detailed energy transfer process is as follows, in which electrons are excited from S0 (ground state) to Sn (high singlet excited state) (4.09 eV) when the N-CD powder is irradiated with 365 nm UV light. The blue fluorescence is attributed to a radiative transition from the lowest singlet excited state (S1 → S0, 2.81 eV). At the same time, the electrons in the S1 state can be transferred to the T1 state (triplet state) by ISC (S1 → T1, ΔEst = 0.46 eV). Furthermore, because of the presence of a large number of subfluorophores in the N-CDs and the destruction of hydrogen bonds in the particles by water, the N-CD solution exhibits fluorescence but no phosphorescence [44].

Next, the phosphorescence stability of the N-CD powder was studied. The phosphorescence intensity of the N-CD powder decreased gradually as irradiation time under UV light (365 nm) increased, with a 10% decrease in intensity observed after 60 min (Fig. 4b). Because hydrogen bonds restricted oxygen penetration into the N-CDs, the phosphorescence intensity of the N-CD powder was almost identical in air and N2 atmospheres (Fig. 4c) [45]. These results revealed that the N-CD powder has excellent phosphorescence stability. Moreover, as vibration effects can quench long-lived triplet excitons, the phosphorescence intensity of the N-CD powder decreased as the temperature increased from 300 to 350 K (Fig. 4d).

Furthermore, because H2O molecules can break hydrogen bonds in the N-CD powder, the presence of water was expected to affect the phosphorescence properties significantly. Therefore, the N-CD powder as probe were applied in the RH. Fig. 5 a shown the afterglow time of the N-CDs-based test paper decreases with the increase of RH. Therefore, the as-prepared N-CD-based test paper can be used as a probe to monitor the RH (0%–100%). The experimental data show that the afterglow time of the N-CDs-based test paper in air is linearly related to the air humidity (y = −0.0729x+7.042, R2 = 0.998) and the RH detection range is 0%–85% (Fig. 5b). Table 1 shows a comparison of the RH results obtained using a hygrometer and the N-CD-based test paper. Notably, the RH determined using the N-CD-based test paper is consistent with that obtained using the hygrometer, which exhibits little relative error. Importantly, the N-CD-based test paper could be reused after drying and maintained a relatively high accuracy for the detection of RH (Fig. 5c). Owing to its portability and accuracy, the N-CD-based test paper is expected to replace conventional hygrometers.

Owing to the excellent RTP properties and good water solubility of the N-CDs, the N-CDs were used as an ink for monochrome printing. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the N-CD-based ink was used to print a digital photograph, which showed good resolution. After the pattern printed on commercial parchment filter paper was completely dry, it was no longer visible. However, under 365 nm UV irradiation, the printed digital photograph emitted blue fluorescence. Furthermore, after removal of the UV light source, the emission from the printed digital photograph changed from blue fluorescence to green phosphorescence. Based on this result, an information encryption and decryption experiment were designed. As shown in Fig. 6b, two-color Arabic numerals were printed on commercial parchment filter paper using two types of CD-based inks (N-CD-based ink with RTP and N-CDs-1-based ink without RTP). Under 365 nm UV irradiation, all the printed Arabic numerals was visible. However, the correct information (N-CD-based ink printing) emerged when the 365 nm UV light was turned off. Based on this result, the RTP N-CDs can be applied as encryption ink in the field of advanced information security.

In summary, the matrix-free RTP N-CDs were successfully fabricated by a hydrothermal method using urea, succinic acid, and acrylamide as starting materials. The as-obtained N-CDs exhibited good biocompatibility and emitted bright blue fluorescence as well as green RTP. The N-CD powder possessed a long phosphorescence lifetime (~158 ms), and the phosphorescence was readily visible to the naked eye for over 7 s under ambient conditions. Because the N-CDs showed good water solubility and RTP properties, the N-CDs could be applied as security ink in information printing and encryption. In addition, as H2O molecules can interrupt the hydrogen bonding in the N-CDs, the afterglow time of the N-CDs decreases in the presence of H2O molecules. Therefore, the N-CD-based test paper for the first time could be used as a probe to rapidly monitor RH (The linear relationship between afterglow time and RH: y = −0.0729x+7.042, R2 = 0.998; the RH detection range is 0%–85%) with precision comparable to that of a hygrometer. Our research provides a new method for the simple and rapid preparation of matrix-free RTP CD materials and expands the application field of RTP N-CDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian (Nos. 2018H0030), and the Education Bureau of Fujian (No. JZ160453). The funder had no role in study design, in the collection, analysis and interpretation of data, in the writing of the report, and nor in the decision to submit the article for publication.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced-performance relative humidity sensor based on MOF-801 photonic crystals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Zhan, Y. Zhu, J. Yan, Y. Chen, Enhanced-performance relative humidity sensor based on MOF-801 photonic crystals, Physics Letters A. 384 (2020) 126678. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physleta.2020.126678.

I.R. Matias, S. Ikezawa, J. Corres, eds., Fiber Optic Sensors, Springer International Publishing, Cham, 2017. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-42625-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fibre-optic sensor technologies for humidity and moisture measurement

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.L. Yeo, T. Sun, K.T.V. Grattan, Fibre-optic sensor technologies for humidity and moisture measurement, Sensors and Actuators A: Physical. 144 (2008) 280-295. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sna.2008.01.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical fiber Fabry–Perot humidity sensor based on polyimide membrane: sensitivity and adsorption kinetics

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide/Cu junction as relative humidity sensor

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chen, M. Zheng, Y. Xiao, H. Dong, H. Zhang, J. Zhuang, H. Hu, B. Lei, Y. Liu, A Self-Quenching-Resistant Carbon-Dot Powder with Tunable Solid-State Fluorescence and Construction of Dual-Fluorescence Morphologies for White Light-Emission, Adv. Mater. 28 (2016) 312-318. https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201503380.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based dots Co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, H. Pang, H.B. Yang, C. Guo, J. Shao, Y. Chi, C.M. Li, T. Yu, Carbon-Based Dots Co-doped with Nitrogen and Sulfur for High Quantum Yield and Excitation-Independent Emission, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 52 (2013) 7800-7804. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201301114.
